featured.title: The Berlin Ringbahn: How to Ride the S41/S42 Circle Line (2026)
featured.summary: Berlin's Ringbahn, the S41 and S42, loops right around the city through 27 stations in about an hour. Here is what the circle line actually is, how to tell which direction each train goes, what ticket covers a full loop, and the stops that are genuinely worth leaving the train for.
featured.alt: A red and ochre Berlin S-Bahn crossing the curving iron Ringbahn bridge over the Oberspree river
featured.label: Newest transport guide

miniPosts.title: How to Use a Berlin Ticket Machine: Buy a BVG Ticket Step by Step (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Standing in front of a Berlin ticket machine, staring at German words while a train pulls in, is a rite of passage. Here is how the BVG machine really works: English menus, the right 2026 fare, how to pay, and the one step that saves you a 60 euro fine.
miniPosts.alt: A yellow BVG ticket machine on a Berlin U-Bahn platform, labelled Fahrscheine Tickets.

miniPosts.title: Berlin Train Announcements Decoded: What the U-Bahn and S-Bahn Voice Is Saying (2026)
miniPosts.summary: The first German you hear in Berlin is a recorded voice on the train. Here is what Zurückbleiben bitte, Nächster Halt, Ausstieg rechts and Ersatzverkehr actually mean, why the S-Bahn plays that little three-note chime, and the one word that tells you the train is about to terminate.
miniPosts.alt: An S-Bahn train arriving under the glass roof of Alexanderplatz station in Berlin

miniPosts.title: Open-Air Cinema in Berlin: How to Catch a Film in English Under the Stars (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Some of the best summer evenings in Berlin happen outdoors, watching a film in a park under the open sky. Here is how to find an open-air cinema showing something in English, which Freiluftkino to pick, and how to plan the night.
miniPosts.alt: The big illuminated screen at Freiluftkino Hasenheide in Neukölln, glowing against the dusk sky

miniPosts.title: How to Spend a Layover in Berlin: What You Can Actually See in a Few Hours (2026)
miniPosts.summary: A long gap between flights at BER can be a real chance to see central Berlin. Here is how much layover time you actually need, how to get in on the S9, where to leave your bag, and the flat historic-core walk you can do in a couple of hours.
miniPosts.alt: Berlin Brandenburg Airport Terminal 1 seen from outside at dusk

posts.title: Where to Watch the 2026 World Cup in Berlin (No, There's No Brandenburg Gate Fan Mile This Year)
posts.summary: The 2026 World Cup is in North America, and Berlin has no Brandenburg Gate fan mile this year. Here is where to actually watch: free screens, beer gardens and bars, with every kick-off in Berlin time.
posts.alt: A summer evening football public viewing in Berlin with a relaxed crowd, outdoor screen and TV Tower skyline

posts.title: Currency Exchange in Berlin: Where to Change Money (and Where Not To) in 2026
posts.summary: Most visitors do not need to exchange cash in Berlin at all. A local guide to getting euros without the hidden margin: bank ATMs, the airport-desk trap, the dynamic-conversion question, and how much cash to actually carry.
posts.alt: Close-up of euro banknotes, a fifty and a twenty, the cash most visitors think they need to exchange before arriving in Berlin

posts.title: Kreuzberg Berlin: A Local Guide to the District's Two Very Different Halves (2026)
posts.summary: Kreuzberg is really two districts sharing one name: loud, Turkish, late-night SO36 in the east, and leafy, café-slow Bergmannkiez in the west. Here is what to see and eat in each half, whether it is safe, and which side fits your day.
posts.alt: The Oberbaumbrücke brick bridge over the Spree connecting Kreuzberg and Friedrichshain in Berlin
